Stephen stands before the council accused of blasphemy, and instead of defending himself with apologies, he tells the story of Israel—a story that becomes an indictment. He begins not with blame but with Abraham, tracing how God made a promise to a man with nothing and no children, a promise that would unfold across generations. But as Stephen recounts this history, something becomes clear: Israel has a pattern. Again and again, God sends deliverers and speaks through them, and again and again, His own people resist. Joseph is sold into slavery by his brothers, yet God uses him anyway. Moses is rejected by those he comes to save—pushed aside with the cruel question, "Who made you ruler and judge?"—yet God appoints him anyway. The people receive the law, the tabernacle, all the visible signs of God's presence, and they respond by turning their hearts back to Egypt, crafting a golden calf while the God who saved them speaks from Mount Sinai. This is not ancient history Stephen is recounting. This is the present moment dressed in historical clothes.

What makes Stephen's speech so devastating is that he never raises his voice in accusation. He simply tells the truth: the people had everything—the presence of God, the words of God, the works of God—and they rejected it. God "turned away from them and gave them over" to worship the very false gods they craved. They were exiled because they would not listen. Stephen then pivots to a question that hangs in the air like a sword: Can a house made by human hands contain the Most High God? The temple stands before them, the pride of Jerusalem, the visible proof of Israel's covenant with God. But Solomon's temple, for all its glory, cannot contain Him who fills all things. The inference is unavoidable. If Israel rejected the God who came to them in the wilderness, who spoke to them through prophets, who filled the tabernacle with His presence, why should they trust in stones and mortar now?

The council will not hear this. They will stop Stephen before he finishes, because they recognize what he is saying: that their resistance to God has not changed. The same stiff-necked refusal that characterized their fathers now characterizes them. Jesus, whom Stephen will soon name, is like Moses—rejected, sent by God, intended as their deliverer. But they, like their ancestors, refuse to obey. As you read this chapter, you must ask yourself the harder question: Where do you resist what God is doing? Not in the dramatic moments of obvious disobedience, but in the daily refusal to believe that God's way is better than your own? In the subtle ways you craft your own gods—your own plans, your own comfort, your own definitions of success—while the true God speaks to you through His Word? Stephen's speech reminds us that religious activity without obedience is not protection; it is deception. The question for you is the same one facing Jerusalem: Will you listen to what God is actually saying, or will you cling to the external forms of faith while your heart turns away?
